title: No Magic Monday This Week Either

comments.content: [IMAGE:0]I'm sorry to cancel the usual question and answer session a second time, but there it is. As I think most of my readers know at this point, my wife Sara is dying; she's been in failing health for a long time, and at this point things are winding up fairly rapidly and I can spare neither the time nor the concentration for the usual flurry of questions. Thank you all for your patience and support, and I'll look forward to an extra lively discussion sometime soon. -- JMG

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
You are both still in my thoughts and prayers, JMG. I also thank you both for showing me beautiful music and the ways of the occultist even in the toughest moments of life.

Martin Hayes and Dennis Cahill, March 5th, 1999. Concert at Modesto, California

Forest (album) by George Winston

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List
comments.content:
Here are all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts. Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment over at the prayer list.

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests.

May JMG's wife Sara Greer, who is dying, make an easy transition into death and be blessed as she moves into the next stage of her spirit's journey. And may John Michael Greer be blessed and soothed, and lent strength in this most difficult time.

May Timmy W. have the guidance, support, and strength he needs to overcome his PTSD, and may his mother, Rachel M., have the energy and wisdom to support him through it.

May Erika's partner James remain cancer free, and make a full return to robust health.

Tyler A's wife Monika's pregnancy is high risk; may Mother and child be blessed with good health and a smooth delivery.

May the surgery for Yuccaglauca's mother Monica's malignant mass be safe, successful, and conclusive of the matter.

May Frank Rudolf Hartman of Altadena California (picture), who is receiving chemotherapy, be completely cured of the lymphoma that is afflicting him, and may he return to full health.

May Audrey's nephew Jon, who is now in a wheelchair due to ALS, have peace and comfort during this difficult time, and be healed of his condition to the greatest degree possible.

May Just Another Green Rage Monster's father, who is dealing with Stage 4 Lymphoma, and mother, who is primary caregiver, be blessed, protected and healed.

May Kyle's friend Amanda, who though in her early thirties is undergoing various difficult treatments for brain cancer, make a full recovery; and may her body and spirit heal with grace.

Lp9's hometown, East Palestine, Ohio, for the safety and welfare of their people, animals and all living beings in and around East Palestine, and to improve the natural environment there to the benefit of all.
 
* * *
Guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ.

If there are any among you who might wish to join me in a bit of astrological timing, I pray each week for the health of all those with health problems on the list on the astrological hour of the Sun on Sundays, bearing in mind the Sun's rulerships of heart, brain, and vital energies. If this appeals to you, I invite you to join me.

replies.title: Re: Ecosophia Prayer List
replies.content: My prayers for all those listed here.

comments.title: Are you coping?
comments.content: How are you holding up? Can we continue to pray for you? Sending love to your wife.

replies.title: Re: Are you coping?
replies.content:
Thanks for asking! I'm doing about as well as can be expected. I've expected something like this to happen for a good long time, and it's been weeks now since it became clear where it was heading; it also helps that I used to work in nursing homes many years ago, so death isn't unfamiliar to me and I know how to care for a dying person. I've done a lot of crying and a lot of processing, and I won't say it's easy but it's gotten easier.

And yes, I'd welcome prayers.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
My thoughts have been with you throughout this process - I have listened to all the music you mentioned that I could access legally and for free. I do not engage in platitudes, so all I can truthfully say is that I wish for a good passing for Sara and a speedy grieving process for you.

With caution about hijacking a post that isn't about me, I lost a near-centenarian member of my family during this period. Her final hours were a race to get enough opiates into her to relieve her agony as something went terribly wrong. On the other hand, without a battery of surgeries, pharmacological interventions, supplementary oxygen and heroic efforts to isolate her during covid, she might have had an easier passing some years ago.

With all my empathy, gratitude and goodwill,
Justin

comments.title: Prayers
comments.content:
My wife and I continue to pray for you both daily.
This is indeed a time of mourning, but do not forget the hope that this is not the end.
Please take all the time you need to deal with Sarah's transition and to process your own grief at separation.
As it is written in Genesis Ch 2, "It is for this reason a man shall leave his father and his mother and be joined to his wife, and they shall become one flesh."

You are genuinely losing a significant part of yourself and it will take time to adjust to the new reality. I imagine it might be similar to becoming an amputee except more so.

May you and Sarah be blessed with mercy and comfort in the days to come.

Christopher Burrell

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'd like to thank everyone for their kind words and prayers. Sara's reached the quiet stage I remember well from thirty-mumble years ago, when I worked in nursing homes: unresponsive but still probably able to hear what's said, needing very little care, far enough along the process of leaving the body that it feels as though she's already at a distance, a dimly seen figure at the edge of sight. It probably won't be long now.

As for me, I've done a lot of grieving and a lot of recollecting; I've drafted a post about Sara, which will be going up on my blog once she's gone; and I've begun making preparations for the next phase of my life. As I noted in an earlier comment, her passing isn't a surprise, but there's still a lot of processing to get ready for being single again at 61 -- and oh bright gods, she was a pack rat. There's an enormous amount of junk she squirreled away for various reasons, which all has to be gone through and mostly donated or thrown out. What has memories connected to it I'll keep or give to her friends, but the local thrift stores are going to be well supplied for a while...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It is a blessing that she is being held metaphorically by your presence and care. She is probably hearing voices from the other side who are there for her. While her impending death is somewhat untimely, it is an nice way to go, in comfortable surroundings with you by her side. So much better than an ICU ward, with harried staff.

I look forward to your tribute to her. Will you also talk about the decision to eschew hospitals? I know that Sara didn't want that but were you afraid that they would try and prolong things to her detriment? As for her being a pack rat, oh dear, she's a woman after my own heart. I will pray for her easeful passing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The decision was entirely Sara's, though I certainly support her in it. Her older brother was in and out of hospitals for years and finally died in a nursing home, and she long ago resolved that she would much rather let go of her life naturally than go through the misery of having it prolonged by medical care.

I'm quite sure she's in contact with people on the other side, btw. That's another of the advantages of being an experienced occultist -- and in her case, she's a conscious medium, one of those unusual people who sees and communicates with disembodied beings without having to go into trance. There'll be a lot of people waiting for her to finally break the silver cord and leave incarnation.
